threats.title: setLockDuration Bypass Allows Immediate Withdrawal of Time-Locked Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: The setLockDuration function validates new unlock time using `block.timestamp + _duration >= old unlockTime` but computes the actual new unlockTime as `lastLockTime + _duration`. As time passes since lastLockTime, the gap between block.timestamp and lastLockTime widens, allowing users to pass the validation check with a small duration that sets unlockTime to a past timestamp, enabling immediate withdrawal.
threats.investigation_rationale: This directly violates the author-stated invariant 'Users must not be able to reduce their previously set lockup times' and enables theft of lockdrop NFT rewards by unlocking before the committed duration expires.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Validation uses `uint32(block.timestamp) + uint32(_duration) < unlockTime` but assignment uses `lastLockTime + uint32(_duration)` — these diverge as time passes

preconditions: User has tokens locked with quantity > 0
Sufficient time has elapsed since lastLockTime (past midpoint of lock duration)
Contract is not paused

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Check: block.timestamp + _duration >= unlockTime. Assignment: unlockTime = lastLockTime + _duration. When block.timestamp >> lastLockTime, small _duration satisfies check but produces past unlockTime.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: User locks 1000 ETH for 365 days during lockdrop, receives NFT rewards. At day 300, calls setLockDuration(66 days): check 300+66=366>=365 passes, but new unlockTime=0+66=day 66 (234 days in the past). Calls unlock immediately.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker retains all lockdrop NFT rewards (worth the full locked amount / nftCost NFTs) while recovering 100% of locked principal after only a fraction of the committed duration.

economic_questions: What is the exact time threshold at which a user can begin reducing their unlock time?
What is the value of NFT rewards relative to the opportunity cost of the shortened lock?
Can this be combined with the phantom remainder carry-over to amplify NFT farming?
validation_requirements: Prove lock-then-early-unlock sequence with specific timestamps in a PoC
Calculate NFT reward value for typical lock amounts against nftCost configurations
Verify that any user past the midpoint of their lock can unlock immediately

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Any amount of lockable tokens (scales with desired NFT count)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All lockdrop NFT rewards for the locked amount, with near-zero effective lock commitment
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Effectively infinite ROI on lock commitment (NFT rewards for free)
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Two transactions: setLockDuration + unlock (can be same block)

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: None — uses user's own locked tokens
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After passing the midpoint of any lock duration
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: No market impact; purely on-chain state manipulation

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Protocol and honest lockers who committed to full lock durations
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Lockdrop NFT dilution proportional to exploiter's share of total locks
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire TVL of locked tokens can be withdrawn early; all lockdrop NFT integrity compromised

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: lastLockTime vs block.timestamp divergence; MaxLockDuration from configStorage
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Prove that for lockDuration D locked at time T0, at time T0+D/2+1, the user can set duration to D/2 and unlock

threats.title: lockOnBehalf Enables Permanent Fund Freezing via Zero-Cost Unlock Timer Reset
threats.hypothesis_description: The _lock function unconditionally overwrites unlockTime to `block.timestamp + lockDuration` for the recipient. Any user can call lockOnBehalf with _quantity=0 (or 1 wei for ETH) for any victim, resetting their unlockTime to the full lock duration from the current timestamp. Repeated calls permanently prevent the victim from ever withdrawing their locked funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: Directly violates the author-stated invariant 'Funds must not get locked forever.' An attacker can grief any user at near-zero cost on Blast L2, permanently freezing potentially large amounts of locked value.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _lock unconditionally sets `lockedToken.unlockTime = uint32(block.timestamp) + uint32(_lockDuration)` without checking if new unlockTime > old unlockTime for existing locks, and lockOnBehalf permits any caller

preconditions: Victim has tokens locked with quantity > 0
Victim has a non-zero lockDuration set in playerSettings (or lockdrop.minLockDuration > 0)
Token used is configured and active
Contract is not paused

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: lockOnBehalf allows _quantity=0. For ERC20: allowance >= 0 always passes. transferFrom(attacker, this, 0) succeeds. unlockTime reset to now + victim's lockDuration.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls lockOnBehalf(tokenContract, 0, victim) just before victim's unlock time expires. Costs only gas (~0.0001 ETH on Blast). Victim's unlockTime extended by full lockDuration. Repeat indefinitely.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack — no direct profit but permanent denial of access to victim's locked funds. Could be weaponized for extortion or competitive sabotage.

economic_questions: Can 0-amount transferFrom succeed for all configured token types?
Does the victim's lockDuration fallback to lockdrop.minLockDuration if unset?
Can an attacker also overwrite a victim's playerSettings.lockDuration via this path?
validation_requirements: PoC: lock 100 ETH as victim, then as attacker call lockOnBehalf(address(0), 0, victim) with msg.value=0
Verify victim cannot unlock after the griefing call
Test with both ETH and ERC20 token paths

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.0001 ETH per griefing call on Blast L2
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit — pure griefing. Victim impact: 100% of their locked balance frozen
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: N/A (griefing); could be monetized via extortion
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single transaction per extension; repeat every lockDuration period

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Just before victim's unlock time expires (maximizes extension per call)
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: No market impact; targeted DoS against specific users

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Any user with locked tokens in the protocol
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire protocol TVL at risk of permanent freezing

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify 0-quantity lock path for both ETH and ERC20; check if _onBehalfOf=address(0) falls through to msg.sender
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace unlockTime changes through lockOnBehalf with 0 quantity

threats.title: Locking Outside Lockdrop Zeroes Accumulated NFT Remainder Destroying User Value
threats.hypothesis_description: In _lock, the local variable `remainder` is initialized to 0. The NFT calculation block (which sets `remainder = quantity % nftCost`) only executes during an active lockdrop. However, `lockedToken.remainder = remainder` is unconditionally executed at the end, zeroing any previously accumulated remainder when a user locks outside a lockdrop period.
threats.investigation_rationale: Users who earned a partial NFT credit during a lockdrop lose it permanently if they make any subsequent lock outside the lockdrop window. This is a silent value destruction that violates user expectations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Local `remainder` initialized to 0 at line ~315, only set inside `if (lockdrop active && sender != migrationManager)` block, but `lockedToken.remainder = remainder` at line ~348 is unconditional

preconditions: User has a non-zero remainder from a previous lockdrop lock
User locks any amount of the same token outside an active lockdrop period
Token is active and configured

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: uint256 remainder; (defaults to 0). Only set inside lockdrop-active block. lockedToken.remainder = remainder; executed unconditionally.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Not attacker-driven — affects any user who locks between lockdrop periods. Value loss per user = remainder * usdPrice (up to nftCost - 1 tokens).
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No attacker profit — pure user value loss. Per configured tokens: up to (nftCost - 1) tokens of NFT progress destroyed per lock.

economic_questions: What is the maximum value of a remainder (nftCost - 1 tokens at current usdPrice)?
How many users are likely to lock between lockdrop periods?
Does the migration path also zero remainder?
validation_requirements: PoC: Lock during lockdrop to get remainder, then lock 1 token outside lockdrop, verify remainder is zeroed
Calculate value per nftCost configuration from deploy scripts

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A — affects normal users, not attacker-driven
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: N/A — user value loss up to (nftCost-1) * usdPrice per instance

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Any user with accumulated remainder who locks between lockdrops
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Up to nftCost-1 tokens worth of NFT progress (e.g., 0.01 ETH at nftCost=1e16)
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Aggregate of all user remainders destroyed over protocol lifetime

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: nftCost per token from configureToken calls; testnet: ETH nftCost=1e16, USDB nftCost=3e18

threats.title: Zero Lock Duration Enables Instant Lock/Unlock Cycle for Reward Gaming
threats.hypothesis_description: When lockdrop.minLockDuration is 0 (no lockdrop configured or stale lockdrop with minLockDuration=0) and a user has not set their own lockDuration, the effective _lockDuration defaults to 0. This sets unlockTime = block.timestamp, allowing immediate unlock within the same block. Since forceHarvest is called in both lock and unlock, the user can accumulate schnibble rewards without meaningful time commitment.
threats.investigation_rationale: The protocol's reward mechanism (schnibbles via forceHarvest) depends on the assumption that locked tokens remain committed for a duration. Zero-duration locks break this assumption and may allow reward extraction without real commitment.
threats.config_evidence: Lockdrop configured via start-lockdrop.ts with minLockDuration parameter (not visible in script); if lockdrop expires or was never configured, lockdrop struct defaults to all zeros including minLockDuration=0

proof_of_signal.pattern: _lockDuration = playerSettings[_lockRecipient].lockDuration; if (_lockDuration == 0) { _lockDuration = lockdrop.minLockDuration; } — both can be 0, leading to unlockTime = block.timestamp

preconditions: No active lockdrop OR lockdrop.minLockDuration == 0
User has never called setLockDuration (playerSettings.lockDuration == 0)
User deploys contract to call lock() then unlock() in same transaction (sequential, not reentrant)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: unlockTime = block.timestamp + 0 = block.timestamp. Unlock check: unlockTime > block.timestamp is FALSE, so unlock allowed same block.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Deploy helper contract that calls lock(token, amount) then unlock(token, amount) sequentially. forceHarvest called in both lock and unlock with locked weighted value briefly non-zero.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Depends on forceHarvest implementation — if rewards calculated per-second, 0 seconds = 0 rewards. If any per-transaction or per-call reward component, may be extractable.

economic_questions: Does forceHarvest grant any rewards for zero-duration locks?
Can flash loans amplify this by locking large amounts briefly?
Does getLockedWeightedValue contribute to reward calculations even within a single block?
validation_requirements: Analyze AccountManager.forceHarvest implementation for time-independent reward components
Test lock+unlock in same block via helper contract
Verify lockdrop struct defaults when no lockdrop configured

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Depends on flash loan availability — potentially large amounts for brief period
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Unknown — depends entirely on forceHarvest reward mechanics

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Flash loan liquidity for the token being locked

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All legitimate stakers (reward dilution if applicable)
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional to attacker's flash loan size vs total locked value

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Lockdrop struct defaults; forceHarvest reward calculation model
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Determine if zero-duration lock yields any non-zero reward from forceHarvest

threats.title: USD Price Governance Vote Exclusivity Violation Enables Double-Counting and Stuck Proposals
threats.hypothesis_description: The disapproveUSDPrice function checks `approvals[msg.sender] == _usdProposalId` to prevent approvers from disapproving, but approveUSDPrice does NOT check `disapprovals[msg.sender] == _usdProposalId`. A price feed operator can disapprove first, then subsequently approve the same proposal, incrementing both counters. This breaks vote mutual exclusivity and can cause proposals to become permanently stuck.
threats.investigation_rationale: This weakens the multisig governance model for price updates. Under specific vote orderings, both approval and disapproval thresholds can be met simultaneously, causing _execUSDPriceUpdate to silently fail and the proposal to become stuck — no execution AND no deletion.

proof_of_signal.pattern: approveUSDPrice checks `approvals[msg.sender] == _usdProposalId` but never checks `disapprovals[msg.sender]`; disapproveUSDPrice sets disapprovals[msg.sender] but doesn't block later approval

preconditions: Active USD price proposal exists
At least one price feed operator can call both disapprove and approve
Vote ordering allows disapprove before approve for at least one operator

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: disapprovalsCount and approvalsCount can both be incremented by the same operator. In _execUSDPriceUpdate: `approvalsCount >= APPROVE_THRESHOLD && disapprovalsCount < DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD` — if both thresholds met, update silently fails.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Stuck proposal scenario: approval threshold reached but disapprovals also at threshold → _execUSDPriceUpdate does nothing, proposal stays (proposer != address(0)), blocking new proposals indefinitely until additional disapprovals force deletion.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Governance DoS — prevents price updates, causing stale prices that distort getLockedWeightedValue and downstream reward calculations.

economic_questions: What specific vote orderings lead to stuck proposals?
Can the stuck state be resolved without admin intervention?
What is the economic impact of stale USD prices on reward distribution?
validation_requirements: Simulate disapprove-then-approve sequence with 5 operators
Identify stuck proposal conditions for default thresholds (3/3)
Quantify reward distortion from stale prices

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: 0 (only gas costs for price feed operators)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.001 ETH gas per vote on Blast L2
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect — stale prices benefit some lockers over others depending on price direction
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Variable — depends on price staleness duration and price movement

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During volatile price movements when stale prices create maximum distortion
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Indirect — affects all users' weighted value calculations

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All protocol users affected by stale token prices
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional to token price movement during stuck period

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Model all vote orderings to find stuck states

threats.title: Fee-on-Transfer Token Accounting Insolvency Leading to Last-Withdrawer Loss
threats.hypothesis_description: The _lock function records `lockedToken.quantity += _quantity` using the requested transfer amount rather than the actual received amount. For fee-on-transfer tokens, the contract receives fewer tokens than recorded. Over time, total recorded quantities exceed the actual contract balance, causing later unlockers to be unable to withdraw their full recorded balance.
threats.investigation_rationale: The project documentation explicitly states the LockManager 'is expected to handle non-standard ERC20 behaviors, including fee-on-transfer.' The current implementation does NOT use a balance-before/after pattern, directly contradicting this stated requirement.
deployment_assumptions: A fee-on-transfer ERC20 token is configured as an active token
threats.config_evidence: Project documentation states fee-on-transfer support is expected. configure-token-test.ts configures USDB, WETH, ETH — whether any of these are fee-on-transfer on Blast depends on the specific token implementations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: token.transferFrom(_tokenOwner, address(this), _quantity) followed by lockedToken.quantity += _quantity — no balance delta check

preconditions: Admin configures a fee-on-transfer token (documentation says this should be supported)
Multiple users lock the fee-on-transfer token
Users begin unlocking their recorded quantities

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: lockedToken.quantity += _quantity records the pre-fee amount. Contract receives _quantity * (1 - feeRate). Deficit per lock = _quantity * feeRate. Cumulative deficit grows with each lock.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: With a 2% fee token: 50 users each lock 100 tokens. Contract records 5000 total, receives 4900. First 49 users unlock 100 each (4900 tokens). Last user has 100 recorded but 0 balance — permanent loss.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Early unlockers can withdraw full recorded amounts while the deficit accumulates on remaining users. An attacker who locks and quickly unlocks extracts more value than they deposited.

economic_questions: Are any Blast-native tokens (USDB, WETH) fee-on-transfer?
What is the rate of insolvency growth per user lock?
Can an attacker intentionally exploit the accounting gap by being an early unlocker?
validation_requirements: Check Blast USDB and WETH contract implementations for transfer fees
Model insolvency accumulation with N users and fee rate F
Verify that unlock transfers the full recorded quantity regardless of contract balance

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Early withdrawer profit = feeRate * total locked by others (accumulates over time)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Proportional to fee rate and number of subsequent depositors
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Gradual insolvency over multiple lock/unlock cycles

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After many users have locked the fee-on-transfer token
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Insolvency affects last N users proportional to accumulated fee deficit

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Last users to unlock the fee-on-transfer token
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Up to 100% loss of locked balance for the very last user
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total fee deficit across all lock operations

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Blast USDB/WETH token semantics; any configured token fee rates
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify contract uses raw _quantity not actual received amount

threats.title: Phantom Remainder After Full Unlock Enables Discounted NFT Minting in Future Lockdrops
threats.hypothesis_description: The unlock function decrements lockedToken.quantity but never clears lockedToken.remainder. A user who accumulated a remainder during a lockdrop can fully unlock all tokens (quantity=0) while retaining their remainder. In a future lockdrop, they can lock a smaller amount that combines with the phantom remainder to mint NFTs at a discount.
threats.investigation_rationale: This violates the invariant that remainder <= quantity. While the user originally 'earned' the remainder through locking, they can reclaim their principal AND keep the NFT credit, effectively double-counting their contribution.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unlock only modifies lockedToken.quantity -= _quantity; lockedToken.remainder is never touched in unlock

preconditions: User has a non-zero remainder from a lockdrop lock
User fully unlocks all tokens after lock duration expires
A subsequent lockdrop occurs for the same token
User re-locks during the new lockdrop (without locking outside lockdrop in between, which would zero the remainder per ECON-H003)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: After unlock: quantity=0, remainder=R. Next lock during lockdrop: quantity = new_amount + R. NFTs = (new_amount + R) / nftCost. User needs (nftCost - R) instead of nftCost for next NFT.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: User locks 199 tokens (nftCost=100) during lockdrop 1 → 1 NFT, remainder=99. Unlocks 199 tokens. In lockdrop 2, locks just 2 tokens → quantity=2+99=101 → 1 NFT. Got NFT for 2 new tokens instead of 100.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Discount of up to (nftCost-1) tokens per NFT. At ETH nftCost=1e16 (~0.01 ETH), the discount is up to ~0.01 ETH per NFT.

economic_questions: What is the NFT value relative to the remainder discount?
Can this be amplified with the setLockDuration bypass (ECON-H001) to farm within single lockdrop?
How many lockdrop cycles can a user abuse this across?
validation_requirements: Verify remainder persists through unlock
Calculate maximum remainder per token configuration
Model multi-lockdrop remainder accumulation scenario

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: nftCost worth of tokens per cycle (returned after unlock)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for lock + unlock per cycle
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to (nftCost-1) tokens discount per NFT per cycle
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on NFT value vs nftCost; potentially significant if NFTs are valuable

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Lock just under 2*nftCost to maximize remainder, then fully unlock
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: NFT dilution for honest lockdrop participants

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Marginal per user — proportional to exploiter's NFT discount share
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: NFT distribution integrity across lockdrop events

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify remainder survives full unlock via PoC

threats.title: Single USD Price Applied to Multiple Tokens Enables Systematic Mispricing
threats.hypothesis_description: The proposeUSDPrice function accepts a single _price and an array of _contracts, and _execUSDPriceUpdate applies this single price to ALL listed contracts. This design makes it impossible to correctly price multiple tokens with different USD values in a single proposal, and a proposer can intentionally include unrelated tokens to apply an incorrect price.
threats.investigation_rationale: getLockedWeightedValue uses usdPrice to compute each user's locked value, which likely determines schnibble reward rates. Applying the same price (e.g., $3000) to both WETH and USDB would 3000x overvalue USDB positions, massively distorting reward distribution.
threats.config_evidence: configure-token-test.ts shows USDB at $1 and WETH at $3000 — these require different prices

proof_of_signal.pattern: _execUSDPriceUpdate loops over usdUpdateProposal.contracts and sets `configuredTokens[tokenContract].usdPrice = usdUpdateProposal.proposedPrice` — same price for all

preconditions: Multiple tokens with different USD values are configured
Price feed operator proposes a single price for multiple token contracts
3-of-5 operators approve the proposal (possibly without checking all listed contracts)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: All contracts in proposal receive identical proposedPrice. If WETH ($3000) and USDB ($1) are both listed with price $3000, USDB lockers get 3000x inflated weighted value.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Colluding price feed operators propose $3000 for both WETH and USDB. Users who locked USDB see their getLockedWeightedValue inflated 3000x, receiving proportionally more schnibble rewards.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker locks USDB (cheap), gets 3000x weighted value, earns disproportionate rewards vs honest WETH lockers.

economic_questions: Does the protocol's operational procedure prevent multi-token single-price proposals?
What is the maximum reward distortion from a mispriced token?
Can approvers verify which contracts are in the proposal before approving?
validation_requirements: Check if approvers can see the full proposal contents (contracts array) on-chain
Model reward distortion with mismatched prices across configured tokens
Verify that proposedPrice applies to ALL contracts in the array

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal USDB to lock (if USDB is the cheap token)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas costs for price proposal workflow
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 3000x reward inflation for USDB lockers
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on reward pool size and relative lock amounts
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Until price is corrected via new proposal

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Minimal — exploit is in price oracle governance, not market manipulation
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When price update needed for one token, sneak in another
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Distorts all reward calculations for affected users

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All lockers of correctly-priced tokens (reward dilution)

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Configured token prices: USDB=$1, WETH=$3000, ETH=$3000
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace getLockedWeightedValue with same usdPrice for different tokens

threats.title: proposeUSDPrice Does Not Execute on Threshold=1, Creating Unreachable Governance State
threats.hypothesis_description: When APPROVE_THRESHOLD is set to 1, the proposer's auto-approval (approvalsCount=1) should trigger execution. However, _execUSDPriceUpdate is only called inside approveUSDPrice, not in proposeUSDPrice. Since the proposer cannot call approveUSDPrice (ProposerCannotApproveError), the proposal requires a second operator to approve, making the effective minimum threshold 2 regardless of configuration.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a logic error where the threshold semantics do not match the implementation. If admin sets threshold to 1 expecting single-operator price updates, the system silently requires 2, potentially blocking time-sensitive price updates.
deployment_assumptions: APPROVE_THRESHOLD is set to 1 via setUSDThresholds
threats.config_evidence: Default APPROVE_THRESHOLD is 3; setUSDThresholds allows admin to set it to 1. No deployment script evidence of this configuration.

proof_of_signal.pattern: proposeUSDPrice increments approvalsCount to 1 but never calls _execUSDPriceUpdate; that function is only called from approveUSDPrice at line 195

preconditions: Admin sets APPROVE_THRESHOLD to 1
Proposer expects their auto-approval to trigger execution

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: approvalsCount++ in proposeUSDPrice reaches 1. If APPROVE_THRESHOLD=1, condition is met but _execUSDPriceUpdate never called.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: No active exploitation — functional bug. Price updates take longer than expected, leading to stale prices.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Stale prices benefit users whose tokens are overvalued relative to current market price.

economic_questions: Is there any deployment scenario where APPROVE_THRESHOLD would be set to 1?
What is the impact of delayed price updates on reward fairness?
validation_requirements: PoC: Set threshold to 1, propose price, verify it is NOT executed
Verify that a second operator must approve to trigger execution

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A — governance bug, not direct exploit
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect — stale prices benefit some lockers

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During volatile markets when price updates are critical
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Delayed price updates affect reward calculations

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users whose tokens are undervalued by stale prices
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Reward pool fairness during delayed update

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Default APPROVE_THRESHOLD=3; admin can change via setUSDThresholds

threats.title: setUSDThresholds Lacks Bounds Validation Enabling Governance Lockout or Bypass
threats.hypothesis_description: setUSDThresholds accepts arbitrary uint8 values for APPROVE_THRESHOLD and DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD without minimum/maximum validation. Setting DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD=0 makes `disapprovalsCount < 0` always false (uint8 underflow semantics in comparison), permanently blocking all price update executions. Setting APPROVE_THRESHOLD=0 with high DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD weakens the multisig to require only 1 approval (proposer's auto-approval, though limited by ECON-H009).
threats.investigation_rationale: An admin error or compromise could permanently brick the price governance system or weaken it to single-operator control, with cascading effects on reward distribution fairness.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setUSDThresholds assigns APPROVE_THRESHOLD = _approve and DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD = _disapprove without bounds checking

preconditions: Admin calls setUSDThresholds with extreme values (0, or > 5)
No active proposal at the time of the call

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD=0: in _execUSDPriceUpdate, `disapprovalsCount < 0` — uint8 comparison, 0 < 0 is false → no proposal can ever execute. APPROVE_THRESHOLD=255: impossible to reach with 5 operators → governance frozen.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Compromised admin sets DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD=0 → all future price updates blocked → prices become permanently stale.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker with admin access can permanently freeze price governance, then exploit stale prices for reward manipulation.

economic_questions: What threshold values render governance non-functional?
Can the admin fix broken thresholds (answer: yes, if no proposal is active)?
What is the recovery path after broken thresholds are set?
validation_requirements: Test _execUSDPriceUpdate with DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD=0
Test with APPROVE_THRESHOLD > 5 (unreachable with 5 operators)
Verify admin can reset thresholds when no proposal exists

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect — permanently stale prices enable reward manipulation
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on price deviation over time

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All protocol users relying on accurate price updates
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional to price divergence over frozen period

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test edge cases: 0, 1, 6, 255 for both thresholds

threats.title: Unbounded configuredTokenContracts Array Growth Enables Gas Exhaustion DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: The configuredTokenContracts array grows with each new token configured via configureToken (tokens are pushed but never removed). This array is iterated fully in setLockDuration, getLocked, and getLockedWeightedValue. With enough configured tokens, these functions exceed the block gas limit, permanently DoSing lock duration changes and reward calculations for all users.
threats.investigation_rationale: getLockedWeightedValue is likely called by external contracts for reward distribution. If it becomes uncallable due to gas limits, the entire reward system breaks. While admin-driven, the array has no bounds check and tokens cannot be removed once added.
threats.config_evidence: configure-token-test.ts adds 3 tokens. No limit visible in code. Admin could add more over time.

proof_of_signal.pattern: configuredTokenContracts.push(_tokenContract) with no upper bound; iterated in setLockDuration (line 241), getLocked (line 378), getLockedWeightedValue (line 400)

preconditions: Admin configures a large number of tokens (estimated 200+ for gas exhaustion)
Users attempt to call setLockDuration or external contracts call getLockedWeightedValue

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Each iteration: storage reads for lockedTokens and configuredTokens (~5000 gas per cold SLOAD). At 200 tokens: ~1M gas per iteration minimum.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin gradually adds tokens over protocol lifetime. At critical threshold, setLockDuration and getLockedWeightedValue become uncallable.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit — DoS of protocol functionality. Users cannot change lock durations; reward calculations break.

economic_questions: What is the realistic gas cost per configured token in each iterating function?
What is the Blast block gas limit?
How many tokens would realistically be configured over the protocol's lifetime?
validation_requirements: Benchmark gas costs of setLockDuration/getLockedWeightedValue with increasing token counts
Determine the threshold at which functions exceed block gas limit
Verify tokens cannot be removed from the array

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Cannot change lock duration; reward calculations may break

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Current token count: 3 (from deploy scripts); Blast gas limit ~30M
